1:
The witnesses treat this opening verse as a model of guided beginnings, and their chief interest is that David, though long since anointed and now with the throne within reach, will not stir a step without asking God. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry makes the theme unhaste: "he that believeth doth not make haste," but waits God's time for the accomplishment of God's promises. David might have summoned all Israel or come in by conquest with the force gathered at Ziklag (1 Chron. 12:22), yet "he that will rule with meekness will not rise with violence." Henry draws the lesson from election: assurance of hope does not slacken but quickens pious endeavour — "If I be elected to the crown of life, it does not follow, Then I will do nothing; but, Then I will do all that he directs me."

The grammatical-historical commentators fix the setting and the means. Keil and Delitzsch read the verse as the header of the whole chapter: David sought the will of the Lord and went up to Hebron, where the men of Judah anointed him, while Abner's rival promotion of Ishbosheth was "an open act of rebellion against Jehovah," who had rejected Saul and chosen David. Charles Ellicott dwells on Hebron itself — one of the most ancient cities of the world, built "seven years before Zoan in Egypt" (Num. 13:22), the long home of Abraham (Gen. 13:18) and the burial place of the patriarchs, a priestly city and the most southerly city of refuge. Adam Clarke, the wesleyan-arminian, adds its natural riches — vineyards, honey, and springs of excellent water.

The one small division concerns the instrument of the inquiry. John Wesley and Keil and Delitzsch say David enquired "by Urim"; the reformed John Gill and, more fully, Henry, Clarke, and Ellicott make it the breastplate of judgment brought by Abiathar the priest, as at 1 Sam. 23:9 — but this is a difference of naming, not of substance, for the Urim was set in that breastplate. All agree, too, on the content of the question: Wesley notes that David "asked not whether he should take the kingdom, for that was appointed before," but only where he should enter upon it, not limiting God yet modestly looking no further than his own tribe. Henry observes that God sent him not to Bethlehem, "little among the thousands of Judah" (Mic. 5:2), but to Hebron, "a little sanctuary" where the sepulchres of the patriarchs would remind him of the ancient promise on which God had caused him to hope.
2:
The narrative now records David's actual ascent, and the witnesses read it as the quiet outworking of the guidance just sought — obedience following inquiry without delay. John Gill, the reformed voice, marks the distance travelled: from Ziklag to Hebron was some sixteen miles, "according to Bunting," a small pilgrimage from the ruins of his exile to the city God had named. The bare movement, "So David went up thither," answers exactly to the word he had received in v.1, so that the verse is less a new episode than the seal of a settled habit: David proves his faith not by speeches but by his feet.

The grammatical-historical commentators supply the family detail that the verse itself insists upon. Keil and Delitzsch set the coming verses in view — the two wives, the men and their households, the anointing, and the message to Jabesh — reading the whole as the orderly founding of a kingdom that begins in the goodness of God, the "grace and truth" later named at v.6 and rooted in the self-revelation of Ex. 34:6. Charles Ellicott simply anchors the two wives, Ahinoam the Jezreelitess and Abigail the widow of Nabal, in their earlier history at 1 Sam. 25:42-43, a reminder that the women who had shared David's wandering now share his settlement.

Though the source here is thin, the witnesses agree on its weight. That David brings his wives with him is, in the reading these commentators invite, the same fidelity that governs the whole chapter: the companions of his tribulation are made companions of his rising. The verse also quietly prepares a later sorrow, for it is at Hebron that David's sons will be born (compare 2 Samuel 3:2), the beginning of a house whose divisions will trouble his reign. For the present, however, the note is one of peaceful establishment. Where Saul had driven David from cave to wilderness, God now gathers him, his household, and his people into a single city; the man who "went up" at the word of the Lord goes up not alone but with all that belongs to him, and the promise long deferred begins, verse by verse, to take visible shape.
3:
The witnesses read this verse as the completion of David's household resettlement and, in it, a small parable of loyalty rewarded. The reformed John Gill fastens on the men "that were with him" — the faithful company who had shared his dangers — and infers that David, once settled on the throne, "no doubt provided well for them," since they had "run the risk of their all on his account." The point is moral as much as historical: those who cast in their lot with David in his rejection are not forgotten in his exaltation.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott gives the concrete scale. David's "whole force of six hundred men, with their families, accompanied him," and they "made their permanent settlement in the towns of the district to which Hebron gave its name." The verse thus records not a royal progress but a migration — an entire community, women and children included, transplanted from Ziklag to the hill country of Judah. What had been a band of outlaws becomes the settled nucleus of a kingdom.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley adds the practical explanation of why the text says "the cities of Hebron" rather than Hebron itself: they "dwelt in the cities or towns belonging to Hebron, which was the Metropolis," for in Hebron proper "there was not space for them all, because it was filled with priests, and with David's court." The observation harmonizes the phrase with the picture of Hebron as a priestly city already drawn at v.1.

There is no debate here; the voices simply converge. Together they present a settlement that is orderly, generous, and complete. David does not enter his first city of dominion as a solitary king but at the head of the people who had suffered with him, each man "with his household," so that the reward of fidelity is corporate and domestic, not merely personal. The scene answers, in its own way, to the pattern the New Testament will draw from such loyalty — that those who "suffer with Christ" shall also "reign with him" (2 Tim. 2:12) — a likeness Henry presses at v.1. The kingdom of David, like the kingdom of David's greater Son, gathers to itself first the tried and the faithful, and gives them a settled place where before they had none.
4:
This verse records the second and public anointing of David, and the witnesses converge on the theme of a kingdom rising by stages. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke supplies the crisp distinction that governs the discussion: the earlier anointing by Samuel gave David jus ad regnum, "a right To the kingdom," while this anointing by Judah gave him jus in regno, "authority Over the kingdom" — the other tribes remaining, for now, "attached to the family of Saul." Right and possession are thus carefully separated, the promise long held becoming at last a limited reality.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott counts the anointings out to three: the private commission of 1 Samuel 16, this recognition by Judah, and the final acceptance by all Israel at 2 Samuel 5:4 — a threefold pattern he sets beside Saul's own private anointing and double public recognition. The men of Judah, he notes, were not only David's tribesmen but knew of his divine selection, had been friendly through his outlawry, and had lately received his gifts (1 Sam. 30:26-31). John Gill adds that it was "the principal of them, the elders of each city," who came.

The reformed and wesleyan-arminian voices weigh the legitimacy and the limits of the act. John Wesley finds it done "upon just grounds," since the kingdom was promised to Judah and David was "designed and anointed by God, whose will both they and all Israel were obliged to obey"; yet he marks their "modesty," that "they make him king of Judah only, and not of all Israel," so that a third anointing was still needed (2 Samuel 5:3), the first (1 Sam. 16:13) having been "only a designation of the person," not "an actual inauguration."

Ellicott then turns to the second half of the verse — the report that the men of Jabesh-gilead had buried Saul. He recalls the old and tragic bond between that town and the tribe of Benjamin: Jabesh had been all but destroyed in the civil war of Judges 21:9-12, its surviving virgins given to the Benjamites, so that a "special connection" existed between Saul the Benjamite and this city, whose men now performed the last kindness to his body. The note is not idle; it sets up David's message of thanks in the verses that follow, and shows the new king honouring the memory of the old even as the crown passes from Saul's house to his own.
5:
The witnesses read David's message to the men of Jabesh-gilead as a window into his magnanimity, and they dwell above all on the largeness of a heart that could honour a dead persecutor. John Gill, the reformed voice, states the plain occasion: David sent "to return them thanks for their courage and boldness in rescuing the bodies of Saul and his sons out of the hands of the Philistines, and for their civility in the burial of them." The kindness David commends is the kindness of the grave — respect paid where nothing more can be gained.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke makes this the moral centre of the verse. He calls it "a generous and noble act, highly indicative of the grandeur of David's mind": David "respected Saul as his once legitimate sovereign" and "loved Jonathan as his most intimate friend," and though Saul "had greatly injured him, and sought his destruction, even this did not cancel his respect for him, as the anointed of God, and as the king of Israel." Clarke reaches for a parallel from his own age, the Huguenot preacher Saurin apostrophizing Louis XIV, his persecutor, as one "whom I once honored as my king, and whom I still reverence as the scourge of the Lord." The point is that reverence for a divine office can survive personal wrong.

John Wesley, more briefly, defines the "kindness" David praises as "this respect and affection," and grounds it in a general principle of humanity: "as it is an act of inhumanity to deny burial to the dead, so it is an act of mercy and kindness to bury them." The care of the dead is thus lifted from mere custom to a work of charity.

The voices do not divide; they unite in reading the verse as David's studied refusal to let old enmity dictate new policy. He might have treated the burial of Saul as a slight to himself, the honouring of a rival house; instead he blesses it. In doing so, the witnesses suggest, David both obeys the instinct of true godliness — which honours the remains, the name, and the family of the departed — and lays, without cynicism, the groundwork of a reign that seeks the good of all Israel. The blessing he pronounces here will broaden into prayer and promise in v.6 and into a bid for their allegiance in v.7.
6:
David's word to the men of Jabesh now passes from thanks into blessing and pledge, and the witnesses read the verse as the meeting of genuine piety with wise policy. John Gill, the reformed commentator, hears in "the Lord show kindness and truth unto you" a prayer for "true kindness" — that God would "confer real favours" and "bestow upon you good and substantial blessings, blessings indeed." The doubling of kindness with truth marks a goodness that is not fleeting but faithful, of a piece with the divine self-description at Exodus 34:6 that Keil and Delitzsch invoke here, where "grace and truth" name "the two sides by which the goodness of God is manifested to men."

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley reads the phrase in the same key: "kindness and truth" means "true and real kindness, not in words only, but also in actions, as you have done to your king." David asks God to answer their deed in the same coin of deeds. Wesley also catches the note of magnanimity in the closing clause: "I will requite you" carries the sense, "So far am I from being offended with you for this kindness to my late enemy" — David turns what might have looked like loyalty to a rival house into an occasion of gratitude.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sharpens both the grammar and the statecraft. The literal rendering, he argues, is not a promise of future recompense but a present act — "I also show you this good" — the Hebrew not conveying directly "the idea of future recompense, as in the English." The thought is that David, "as now the rightful king of Israel, appreciates the act, and wishes to show publicly his favour to the men of Jabesh-gilead." Ellicott is candid that this is "quite in accordance with his whole bearing towards Saul and his house, and, at the same time, was one of wise policy," for in the next verse David "suggests the propriety of their now recognising him as the successor of their lost monarch and friend."

So the witnesses hold two things together without embarrassment. David's kindness is real, the overflow of a heart that honours God's anointed even when that anointed had hunted him; and it is also politic, the first move of a king who would gather to himself the loyalty of a brave and grateful people. There is no debate among them, only a shared recognition that in David, as often in Scripture, sincere devotion and sound prudence are not enemies but allies.
7:
With this verse David's message to Jabesh reaches its point: a call to courage and a bid for allegiance. The reformed John Gill reads "let your hands be strengthened, and be ye valiant" as an antidote to fear — the men of Jabesh might well dread Philistine reprisal "who might resent their conduct in taking away from them the bodies of Saul and his sons, whom they had hung up in triumph," and so David "bids them be of good heart and courage, and not be afraid of them." The exhortation is pastoral before it is political: David steadies a frightened people.

The wesleyan-arminian voices press the strategic reading. Adam Clarke observes plainly that "David certainly wished to attach the men of Jabesh to his interest," seeing that "they were generous and valiant, and must be of great service to him whose part they espoused"; and he names David's real anxiety — "that they would attach themselves to the house of Saul, in consideration of the eminent services Saul had rendered them in rescuing them from Nahash, king of the Ammonites." The old debt to Saul, that is, made Jabesh a natural stronghold of Saul's dynasty, and David moves to win it before Abner can. John Wesley catches the double note of the verse: "be not afraid lest the Philistines should punish you for this fact, but take good courage, I will defend you"; and he softens the blunt reminder that "your master Saul is dead" into a word of comfort — "though your master Saul be dead, and so your hearts may faint, as if you were now sheep without a shepherd," yet David offers himself as their new protector.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch let the verse run straight into its dark counterpart, joining David's appeal — "your master Saul is dead, and also the house of Judah have anointed me king over them" — to the notice that "Abner the son of Ner, captain of Saul's host, took Ishbosheth, and brought him over to Mahanaim." The juxtaposition is deliberate: at the very moment David reaches out to unite Israel around himself, another hand is at work to divide it.

The witnesses do not contend here. They agree that David's message is at once brave-hearted and shrewd — comforting the fearful, honouring the dead, and quietly commending himself as the true successor. Yet they also see, with Keil and Delitzsch, that David's open and generous bid for the north will be met not by consent but by the rival kingship Abner is even now setting up, so that the peace David seeks must give way, for a season, to the war the next verses describe.
8:
Here the chapter's great rivalry opens, and the witnesses divide the field between the man God made king and the man Abner made king. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry states the theme most fully: one would have thought David would come to the throne unopposed, "since all Israel knew how manifestly God had designated him to it," yet "such a spirit of contradiction is there in the devices of men to the counsels of God" that "such a weak and silly thing as Ishbosheth" is set up to reign instead. Henry makes David's kingdom typical of the Messiah's, "against which the heathens rage and the rulers take counsel" (Ps. 2:1-2). The prime mover is Abner, acting "in his zeal for the lineal succession" or "rather in his affection to his own family," and, above all, "because he had no other way to secure to himself the post of honour he was in, as captain of the host." Henry's verdict is sharp: "See how much mischief the pride and ambition of one man may be the occasion of. Ishbosheth would never have set up himself if Abner had not set him up, and made a tool of him."

The reformed John Gill and the wesleyan-arminian John Wesley agree on Abner's motive. Gill notes that Ner was Saul's uncle and Abner therefore Saul's cousin and his army's general, so that "willing to keep" his command was reason enough for the deed. Wesley is blunter: Abner acted "though ambition and desire of rule, because he knew that Ishbosheth would have only the name of king, whilst he had the power."

The grammatical-historical commentators fill in the persons and places. Charles Ellicott identifies Abner as Saul's cousin-german and commander (1 Sam. 14:51), reckons that "four or five years" had passed since Saul's death, and unfolds the name Ishbosheth: called "Eshbaal" (the fire of Baal) in 1 Chronicles 8:33, the element "Baal" was later changed to "bosheth" (shame), whether to mark opposition to idolatry or "to mark God's utter rejection of the house of Saul." Keil and Delitzsch read the same substitution, comparing how Baal became "Bosheth" in Hosea 9:10 and Jeremiah 3:24. Mahanaim, both note with Ellicott, was the town famous from Jacob's vision (Genesis 32:2), a Levitical city safe from the Philistines beyond Jordan — later David's own refuge from Absalom (2 Samuel 17:24). The witnesses do not debate; they agree that the whole rival monarchy is Abner's contrivance, a human counsel raised, for a time, against the plain counsel of God.
9:
This verse charts the reach of Abner's puppet-kingdom, and the witnesses spend their labour chiefly on its geography and on what the list of territories reveals. John Gill, the reformed voice, begins east of the Jordan: Ishbosheth was made king "over Gilead, which lay beyond Jordan, and included the tribes of Gad and Reuben, and the half tribe of Manasseh," peoples Abner "prevailed upon to acknowledge Ishbosheth their king."

The grammatical-historical commentators read the sequence of names as a map of gradual conquest. Charles Ellicott argues that the verse "expresses the gradual extension of Ishbosheth's dominion as the country became freed from the Philistines": authority was established first "over Gilead — i.e., the country on the east of the Jordan," then "over the Ashurites," then southwards "over Jezreel, the wide plain between the mountains of Gilboa and the little Hermon," then "over Ephraim, including the half-tribe of Manasseh," then "over Benjamin," and "finally, over all Israel, excepting, of course, Judah." Keil and Delitzsch likewise let the list stand as the frame of the coming conflict, with the pointed closing clause the whole chapter turns upon: "But the house of Judah followed David."

The one real crux is the word "Ashurites." The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley takes it, "as the Chaldee paraphrast and others understand it," for "the tribe of Asher," and glosses Jezreel as "a large and rich valley on the borders of Zebulun, Issachar and Naphtali, put for them all," and Gilead as "largely so taken, for all the land of Israel beyond Jordan." Ellicott agrees that "no satisfactory explanation of this name as it stands has been found, but it is probably meant for Asherites," the reading of some manuscripts and of the Chaldee. Adam Clarke is more cautious, allowing only that they were "probably men of the tribe of Ashur," and he draws out the political meaning of the whole verse: Abner "was afraid to bring the new king to any place where he was likely to meet with much resistance, till he had got his army well recruited," the Philistines still holding the principal towns after their victory at Gilboa.

So the witnesses agree on the substance while differing only over one obscure name. Ishbosheth's realm, on their reading, is a thing assembled piecemeal and from weakness — a dominion pieced together as the Philistine grip loosened, claiming "all Israel" on paper while the one tribe that mattered, Judah, quietly held to the king whom God had chosen. The grand catalogue of territories, set against that final short clause, only underscores how the counsel of man overreaches even as the counsel of God goes quietly forward.
10:
The verse gives the ages and reigns of the two kings, and the witnesses wrestle mainly with an arithmetical puzzle: how can Ishbosheth have reigned only "two years" while David reigned seven and a half at Hebron? John Gill, the reformed commentator, notes simply that Ishbosheth "was forty years old when he began to reign, being born the same year his father began to reign," anchoring the man in the chronology of Saul's own accession.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke lays out the difficulty and the proposed solutions at length. It is well observed, he says, "that Ish-bosheth reigned all the time that David reigned in Hebron, which was seven years and six months," so the "two years" must be understood carefully. He canvasses several readings: that Ishbosheth "reigned two years before any but the tribe of Judah had attached themselves to the interest of David"; that "Abner in effect reigned the last five years," Ishbosheth having "only the name of king after the first two"; or that the sense is, "when Ish-bosheth had reigned two years over Israel, he was forty years of age." He records Houbigant's more drastic remedy — emending "two years" to "six years" (shishshith for shetayim), a reading he says nine or eleven manuscripts support with "shanah" — but notes that "the number two is acknowledged by all the ancient versions, and by all the MSS. yet collated." Clarke's own preference is that the writer dates from the campaign that follows: Ishbosheth "had reigned two years without any rupture with David" until Abner led Israel across Jordan to Gibeon and the battle of these verses broke out.

John Wesley reaches the same resolution far more briefly, glossing "two years" as "namely, before the following war broke out, which continued five years." The two Wesleyan voices thus agree that the two years mark the peaceful interval, not the whole reign, with the remaining span filled by the long war of 2 Samuel 3:1.

The debate here is genuine but bounded, and it is a debate about reckoning rather than doctrine. All the witnesses accept the plain text that David "was king in Hebron over the house of Judah seven years and a half," as Keil and Delitzsch insist against those who would divide it into a two-year and a five-and-a-half-year phase. The disproportion between the two reigns is left, in the end, as evidence of the very thing the chapter is showing: that Ishbosheth's kingship was a brief and troubled thing, while "the house of Judah followed David" from first to last.
11:
This short verse records the length of David's Hebron reign — "seven years and six months over the house of Judah" — and the witnesses treat it as the fixed chronological peg from which the surrounding difficulties are measured. John Gill, the reformed voice, adds only the pointed qualification that David was king in Hebron "over the house of Judah, and over them only," keeping before the reader the fact that the kingdom is as yet divided and the greater part of Israel still withheld.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch make the verse the hinge of the chapter, letting it run on into the outbreak of war. The figure of seven years and six months, they insist, must be taken at face value against harmonizing schemes that would split it, and it is precisely this settled span that sets Ishbosheth's bare "two years" (v.10) in such striking relief. They then trace the movement that turns rivalry into open conflict: "When Abner had brought all Israel under the dominion of Ishbosheth, he also sought to make Judah subject to him," and so "went from Mahanaim to Gibeon" — "the present Jib, in the western portion of the tribe of Benjamin, two good hours to the north of Jerusalem" — taking the fighting men of Ishbosheth with him. There Joab advanced to meet him with the warriors of David, and the two armies encamped on opposite sides of "the pool of Gibeon," probably "one of the large reservoirs that are still to be found there."

The witnesses, few as they are on this verse, do not divide. They read it as a deliberate timestamp by the sacred historian: the reign at Hebron is measured out exactly, so that the reader may hold the whole rival period in view and see how long the patience of David was tried before the promise of an undivided throne was fulfilled. The half-year beyond the seven is not idle precision; it fixes the duration of a kingdom that was real but partial, godly but contested. And by carrying the notice straight into Abner's march on Gibeon, the historian shows that the count of years is no mere record-keeping but the frame of a struggle — the settled, God-given reign of Judah's king set over against the restless, man-made claim that now advances to force the issue by the sword. The next verses will bring the two hosts to the fatal pool.
12:
The armies now take the field, and the witnesses concentrate on the meeting-place and the intentions that brought the two captains to it. John Gill, the reformed commentator, identifies the aggressor plainly: "Abner the son of Ner," who had been "captain of Saul's host, and now of Ishbosheth's," leads out "the servants," that is the fighting men, of the northern king. The initiative, the witnesses agree, lies wholly on Ishbosheth's side; David's men will only respond.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott gives Gibeon its history and its strategic weight. It was, he recalls, the city famous in the conquest as "the only city which succeeded, though by craft, in making a league with the conquerors" (Joshua 9), lying "five and a half miles north-west from Jerusalem, and at a long distance both from Mahanaim and from Hebron." Because it sat far from either capital, Ellicott weighs why the generals should meet there at all, and offers his judgement: the encounter was "possibly by design, but more likely each engaged in the effort to extend their respective masters' sway over the tribe of Benjamin." Gibeon, that is, was contested ground in the borderland the two kingdoms were both reaching for. Keil and Delitzsch, tracing the same march "from Mahanaim to Gibeon," read Abner's movement as the deliberate attempt "to make Judah subject" to Ishbosheth now that all Israel had been brought under him.

The witnesses do not dispute one another here; their small difference is only one of emphasis — whether the confrontation was arranged or arose from converging ambitions over Benjamin. What unites them is the recognition that Abner is the one who "went out" first, carrying the war into the very tribe of Saul, where he might expect the warmest support. The pool of Gibeon, which the next verse will name as the place where the two hosts sit down face to face, becomes on their reading the theatre chosen by the house of Saul, not by the house of David — a point the commentators will make much of when the blame for the bloodshed is weighed at v.27. For now the verse simply marches the rival forces to their fatal meeting, the servants of a king Abner made against the servants of the king God made.
13:
The two armies now come face to face, and the witnesses attend to the commander who meets Abner and to the place where they halt. John Gill, the reformed voice, names him: "Joab the son of Zeruiah, a sister of David," who "was general of David's army." The verse thus sets cousin against cousin's cause, David's nephew leading the men of Judah as Abner leads the men of Israel.

The grammatical-historical commentators enrich both the man and the setting. Charles Ellicott recalls that Zeruiah was David's sister (1 Chronicles 2:16) and Joab "the most prominent of her three distinguished sons," who would afterward, "by his successful leading of the forlorn hope in the siege of Jerusalem" (1 Chronicles 11:6), become "permanently established as commander-in-chief of David's army." He describes the pool of Gibeon itself as "a large reservoir or tank, arranged to store the overflow from a subterranean reservoir fed by a spring," whose ruins "still remain, about 120 feet long by 100 broad," and he pictures the scene exactly: "the hostile forces halted in full sight of each other on the opposite sides of the pool." Keil and Delitzsch describe the same tableau — "they sat down, the one on the one side of the pool, and the other on the other side" — and read forward into Abner's proposal, taking the phrase "play before us" as "the war-play of single combat."

John Wesley, the wesleyan-arminian voice, glosses the verb of meeting simply: the two hosts "met the opposite army, and put themselves in a posture for battle." The commentators do not divide; they agree in the picture of two armies drawn up on either bank of the reservoir, halted and watching, the water between them lending a strange pause before the violence. That pause, the witnesses observe with Henry at v.8, "gave both sides time to deliberate" — and it is into this deliberate stillness that Abner will drop his fatal proposal in the next verse, turning what might have ended in a standoff into the deadly sport of Helkath-hazzurim. The verse is thus a hinge: the armies are in place, the leaders named, the ground surveyed, and the reader waits, with the two hosts, to see who will first draw the sword.
14:
Abner's proposal — "let the young men now arise, and play before us" — draws the sharpest moral censure in the chapter, and the witnesses are nearly united in condemning it. John Gill, the reformed voice, sets the scene by noting Joab's restraint: Abner spoke because Joab "made no motion towards an engagement," "his orders from David being only to act on the defensive, and avoid as much as possible the effusion of blood." The contrast between the two captains is drawn from the first.

The wesleyan-arminian commentators are the most severe on Abner's word "play." Adam Clarke calls it "diabolical play, where each man thrust his sword into the body of the other, so that the twenty-four fell down dead together." John Wesley expands the charge into a portrait: Abner "speaks like a vain-glorious and cruel man, and a soldier of fortune, that esteemed it a sport to see men wounding and killing one another." Wesley grants that the combat served a double purpose — "partly for their mutual recreation and trial of skill, and partly that they might be engaged in a battle" — but ends with the verdict the whole tradition endorses: "he is unworthy the name of a man, who is thus prodigal of human blood." Matthew Henry, at v.8, had already sounded the same note — "Fools thus make a mock at sin" — and faulted Joab too, who "had not resolution enough to resist" the challenge, standing on "a point of honour."

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott is alone in reading Abner's motive more charitably. He suggests the proposal was meant "to avoid unnecessary bloodshed between the tribes of a common parentage, and also, perhaps, to prevent the weakening of the nation in the face of their common Philistine foe" — a contest "decided by a combat between a few champions chosen on either side." He aptly compares the duel to that of the Horatii and Curiatii, "under strikingly similar circumstances and with similar results, as described by Livy." Keil and Delitzsch likewise take Abner's aim to be "probably for the purpose of avoiding an actual civil war."

So the witnesses genuinely differ over the man's intent. The Wesleyan and Puritan voices see wanton cruelty, a general who "made a jest of bloodshed"; the grammatical-historical readers allow the darker reading but suggest a soldier's calculation to limit slaughter. What none of them defends is the result: the "play" that ends in twenty-four corpses. And whatever Abner intended, Henry's later observation stands over the scene — that those "forward to make mischief are commonly the first to complain of it," as Abner himself will, once the sword he made light of turns against his own men.
15:
The champions are chosen and step forward, and the witnesses read the verse as the grim answer to Abner's challenge — the "play" of v.14 now made flesh. John Gill, the reformed commentator, notes that the "twelve of Benjamin" were men "whom Abner had picked out of that tribe, being his own, and whom he knew to be stout and courageous men, and closely attached to him." The number is matched exactly on the other side — "twelve of the servants of David" — so that the contest is deliberately even, tribe against tribe, twelve against twelve.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch supply the sober framing, letting the verse run on into its bloody sequel: the twelve "went over," that is "went out of the two camps to the appointed scene of conflict," and there "one seized the other's head, and his sword was immediately in the side of the other, so that they fell together." They read the participial clause "and his sword was in the side of the other" as circumstantial — each combatant's blade already thrust home in his antagonist even as he grasped his head — a picture of instantaneous, mutual slaughter. The very balance of the champions, twelve drawn from Benjamin for Ishbosheth and twelve from David's men, ensures that the duel decides nothing, for they are too equally matched to leave a victor.

Here the source is spare, and the witnesses do not multiply comment or dispute; the verse is a hinge between the challenge and its horror. Yet the little that is said carries weight. That Abner hand-picked his twelve from his own tribe, men bound to him by kinship and loyalty, only deepens the tragedy the next verse will name: these are not strangers thrown together but the flower of Benjamin, chosen for their courage and sent to kill and die in a general's game. The careful symmetry of the numbers — twelve and twelve, going over "by number" — lends the scene the air of a rite, a formal combat entered with full deliberation on both sides. And because the match is so exact, the reader already senses what v.16 will confirm: that such equal valour can end only in equal death, and that the "play" Abner proposed will settle the war no more than it spares the men who wage it. The twelve rise; the reader waits for them to fall.
16:
The champions destroy one another to the last man, and the witnesses read the verse for both its ghastly detail and the name it left behind. John Gill, the reformed voice, describes the grip: they "caught every one his fellow by the head," that is "by the hair of his head with his hand," each seizing his opposite before driving the sword home. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke supposes it may have been "by the beard, if these persons were not too young to have one," and reaches for a martial parallel — Alexander's order that the Macedonians shave their beards, since, as he told Parmenio, "in battle there is no better hold than the beard." John Wesley agrees it was "by the hair of the head, which after their manner was of a considerable length."

The whole interest of the interpreters, however, gathers on the strange place-name the slaughter earned: Helkath-hazzurim. The witnesses offer a small cluster of renderings. Clarke reports the range: "The portion of the mighty," or, following the Targum, "The inheritance of those who were slain." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes that the margin gives "the field of strong men," but judges the etymology "very doubtful," adding that "most modern expositors understand it as meaning the field of sharp edges." Wesley prefers "the field of rock," and draws out the sense with evident admiration: it commemorates "men who stood like rocks unmovable, each one dying upon the spot where he fought." Matthew Henry, at v.8, had gathered the same thought — "the field of rocky men, men that were not only strong in body, but of firm and unshaken constancy, that stirred not at the sight of death."

So the debate here is small and purely lexical: whether the name honours the combatants' might, mourns the slain, marks the sharpness of the swords, or memorializes the rock-like firmness of men who would neither flee nor yield. The witnesses do not quarrel over it; they lay the possibilities side by side. What they agree upon is the pity beneath the valour. Henry catches it best when he calls such honour "poor honour for men to purchase at so vast an expense," and sets against it the promise that "those that lose their lives for Christ shall find them." The twenty-four fall "down together," so evenly matched that the combat Abner devised to decide the war decides nothing at all — a field of rock indeed, but a field that only opens the way to the general battle of v.17.
17:
The single combat having settled nothing, the two hosts join in earnest, and the witnesses read this verse as the true turning-point of the day. John Gill, the reformed commentator, marks the transition: "when the twenty-four men fell together, and no decision could be made thereby, or any triumph on either side, both armies drew up in battle array, and fought very furiously." The deadlock of Helkath-hazzurim gives way to a general engagement, "a very sore battle," in which Abner and the men of Israel are beaten before the servants of David.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott weighs the scale of the fighting and its significance. The numbers engaged, he judges, were "probably not large, as the whole number of the slain was nineteen on David's side, and 360 on that of Israel" — figures the chapter will give at vv.30-31. Yet small as it was in casualties, "it was, however, a turning-point in the struggle." Keil and Delitzsch read the same movement, calling it "a general and very sore, or fierce, battle, in which Abner and his troops were put to flight by the soldiers of David," and they single out the one incident the historian will dwell upon — the slaying of Asahel by Abner — as the event "mentioned here on account of the important results which followed." Matthew Henry, at v.8, had already drawn the providential lesson: the first combat "was a drawn battle, in which all were killed on both sides," but in this second and decisive trial "those that gave the challenge went away with loss," for "David had God on his side; his side therefore was victorious."

The witnesses do not divide here; they agree that the rout of Abner's forces is both militarily modest and historically weighty. The bloodshed is real but limited — a mercy the commentators will credit to David's defensive restraint — yet the outcome tilts the long contest decisively toward the house of Judah. Ellicott's phrase captures the shared reading: a turning-point. What began as Abner's aggressive march on Gibeon and his cruel "play" of champions ends in the defeat of the very man who provoked it, so that the sword he made light of drawing recoils upon his own army. The victory is God's vindication of his chosen king, quietly given through the valour of David's tried men, and it prepares the desperate pursuit — and the fatal encounter of Asahel and Abner — that the following verses relate.
18:
With Abner in flight the historian turns to a single pursuit, and the witnesses gather around the tragedy of Asahel — the swift young warrior undone by his own gift. John Gill, the reformed voice, and Keil and Delitzsch, the grammatical-historical readers, set the scene: the three sons of Zeruiah — Joab, Abishai, and Asahel — were all in the battle, and Asahel "was as light of foot as a wild roe," and so pursued Abner without turning "to the right hand nor to the left."

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry gives the fullest reading, and it is a study in the perils of pride. Asahel, "famous for swiftness in running," aimed at no lesser prize than Abner himself, "either slain or bound," thinking it "would put an end to the war and effectually open David's way to the throne." Henry judges the design "brave, had he been par negotio — equal to its accomplishment," but faults its rashness: Asahel was "careless of the opportunities he had of seizing others," his eye fixed on Abner alone, and so "let not the swift man glory in his swiftness, any more than the strong man in his strength — magnis excidit ausis, he perished in an attempt too vast for him." From this Henry draws the two lessons the whole tradition echoes: "how death often comes upon us by ways that we least suspect," and how "we are often betrayed by the accomplishments we are proud of." Asahel's very swiftness "did him no kindness, but forwarded his fate, and with it he ran upon his death, instead of running from it."

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke supplies the martial background that makes Asahel's fame intelligible. To be "swift of foot was deemed a great accomplishment in the heroes of antiquity," and he recalls Homer's standing epithet for Achilles, "the swift-footed," given "no less than thirty times in the course of the Ilias," adding that speed "had a qualification also among the Roman soldiers, who were taught both to run swiftly, and to swim well."

The witnesses do not contend; they agree in reading Asahel as a warning as much as a hero. Henry also marks the generosity that will appear in the next verses — Abner "giving him notice of the danger he exposed himself to" — and finds in the older captain's reluctance "to incur Joab's displeasure" a sign that Abner "really believed David would have the kingdom at last," and so, in opposing him, "acted against his conscience." The stage is thus set: a gallant youth, proud of a gift that will destroy him, closing on a seasoned soldier who does not wish to kill him.
19:
The chase narrows to a single line, and the witnesses read Asahel's headlong pursuit as valour uncooled by prudence. John Gill, the reformed voice, sees him "ambitious of the glory of taking or slaying the general of the army of Israel," but faults his reckoning: he was "trusting to his swiftness, not considering that the race is not always to the swift, and that he had to do with a veteran soldier, and he a raw young man, though valiant." The mismatch is the point — youthful speed against experienced arms.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott fills in both the man and the mechanics of what is coming. Asahel, "the youngest of the three nephews of David," took part in the battle with his elder brothers, and "well knowing how completely the cause of Ish-bosheth depended upon Abner, pertinaciously sought him out in the pursuit." His "great fleetness enabled him to overtake Abner and, coming behind him, endanger his life." Ellicott then anticipates the fatal blow of v.23, explaining the weapon: Abner slew him "with the hinder end of his spear," which was "sharpened for the purpose of sticking it into the ground," as at 1 Samuel 26:7. He also corrects a rendering, noting that "the fifth rib," here and elsewhere (2 Samuel 3:27; 2 Samuel 20:10), "should be translated abdomen." And he weighs Abner's motive in the pursuit: his "reluctance to kill Asahel may have been partly on account of his extreme youth, but was chiefly through dread of the vengeance of Joab."

The witnesses do not divide over this verse; they read together a portrait of single-minded courage running toward disaster. What Gill states as a proverb — the race is not to the swift — Ellicott confirms in the event: Asahel's fleetness, his one great gift, brings him within reach of a man he cannot master, and delivers him not to glory but to death. The verse also quietly begins to complicate the reader's sympathies, for Abner, the aggressor of the day and the maker of the rival king, is here the one who does not wish to strike, hunted by a youth who "turned not to the right hand nor to the left." That reluctance, and the vengeance Abner rightly fears from Joab, will echo through the chapters to come; but for now the interpreters simply mark the tragedy tightening — a raw young hero, blind to his own limits, closing on a veteran who would rather warn him than wound him.
20:
This briefest of verses — Abner looking back and asking, "Art thou Asahel?" — draws only a spare word from the witnesses, yet they read even this glance as freighted with the coming tragedy. John Gill, the reformed commentator, offers the plain sense: Abner, "perceiving one at his heels, and making haste up to him," turns to see who presses so close behind. The question is not idle curiosity but the pause of a seasoned soldier taking the measure of his pursuer.

Read in the light of what precedes and follows, the moment is charged. Abner has been fleeing a lost field; a single runner has outstripped the rest and gained upon him, and the veteran turns to identify the man whose speed alone could have brought him so near. That he asks the name at all, rather than striking at once, already shows the reluctance the commentators noted at v.19 — Abner would know whom he faces before he acts, and the answer, "I am," will confirm his fear. For the man behind him is not a common soldier to be cut down without consequence, but Asahel, the brother of Joab, whose death Abner "dreaded" for the vengeance it must bring.

The witnesses do not multiply comment here, and rightly, for the verse is a hinge and not a resting-place. Its whole weight lies in the recognition it records: the pursuer is named, the pursued knows him, and the knowledge changes everything. Where Abner might have dispatched an anonymous enemy without a second thought, the naming of Asahel turns the encounter into a personal crisis — the older captain must now either be killed by a youth he could master, or kill a youth whose brother will never forgive him. That is precisely the dilemma Abner will lay before Asahel in the next verse, begging him to turn aside and seize a lesser prey. The single question of v.20, then, is the moment the trap closes: not a trap Asahel has sprung on Abner, but one that Asahel's own headlong courage has sprung on himself. The veteran looks back, sees who it is, and knows — as the reader now knows — that only one of them is likely to leave the field alive, and that the blood shed here will not be quickly buried.
21:
Abner, recognizing his pursuer, pleads with Asahel to turn aside, and the witnesses read the verse as an act of mercy pressed on a young man too proud to receive it. John Gill, the reformed voice, grounds Abner's appeal in mingled motives — "having a respect for him, and consulting his good, and however fearing his brother Joab should he slay him." The plea, that is, springs partly from genuine goodwill and partly from dread of the vengeance a Joab would exact.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch paraphrase Abner's counsel plainly: "Turn to thy right hand or to thy left, and seize one of the young men and take his armour for thyself" — that is, "slay one of the common soldiers, and take his accoutrements as booty, if thou art seeking for that kind of fame." Abner offers the youth an honourable prize within his reach, so as not to have to kill him; and, as the following verse will show, he repeats the warning "out of regard for Joab and their former friendship." The wesleyan-arminian voices fill in the same sense. Adam Clarke observes that "Asahel wished to get the armor of Abner as a trophy," a thing "greatly coveted by ancient heroes," and that Abner, "wishing to spare him, for fear of exciting Joab's enmity," urged him to a lesser quarry. John Wesley renders Abner's meaning as a soldier's advice: "If thou art ambitious to get a trophy or mark of thy valour, desist from me who am an old and experienced captain, and go to some young and raw soldier; try thy skill upon him, and take away his arms from him."

The witnesses do not divide; they agree that Abner does everything a merciful enemy can do short of surrender. Matthew Henry, at v.18, had drawn the wider lesson from this generosity — that "it is wisdom in all contests to compare our own strength with that of our adversaries," lest we prove "enemies to ourselves" (Luke 14:31) — and had found it "commendable for enemies to be thus respectful one to another." The pathos of the verse lies in the refusal that closes it: "But Asahel would not turn aside from following of him." Every warning is offered, every door of escape held open, and the young man walks past them all. Abner's plea, on the witnesses' reading, thus fixes the responsibility for what follows: the veteran sought peace, and the blood about to be shed will be laid, in the reckoning of the chapters ahead, not at the door of the man who begged, but of the youth who would not hear.
22:
Abner warns Asahel a second time, and the witnesses read this verse as the last open door before the fatal stroke — mercy repeated and, tragically, refused. John Gill, the reformed commentator, catches the reluctance in the very repetition: Abner "said again to Asahel," being "loath to dispatch him." That a hard-pressed captain in flight should pause twice to plead with his pursuer is, on Gill's reading, the mark of a man unwilling to shed this particular blood.

The verse's force lies in the question Abner puts, which the grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch preserve and unfold at v.21: "Wherefore should I smite thee to the ground? how then should I hold up my face to Joab thy brother?" The appeal is twofold — a plea for Asahel's own life and a confession of Abner's dread of the consequences. To kill Asahel would be to make an implacable enemy of Joab, and Abner, "out of regard for Joab and their former friendship," shrinks from the deed. The witnesses stress that this dread proves prophetic: the very vengeance Abner fears here will overtake him, for this slaying "cost Abner his life," as Clarke notes at v.21, in the chapter that follows (2 Samuel 3:27).

The witnesses do not divide over so brief a verse; they read it as of a piece with the mercy of the preceding lines. Abner has already offered Asahel an easier prize; now he spells out, in as many words, the plain arithmetic of the moment — that the veteran can kill the youth, does not wish to, and warns him of exactly what will happen if he presses on. The pathos deepens precisely because the warning is so clear and so kindly meant. Asahel is not ambushed or deceived; he is told, twice, that he is running upon his death, and told by the man who would rather not deal it. The verse thus completes the case the commentators have been building since v.19: the responsibility for Asahel's fall lies with Asahel's own unbending pride, not with Abner's arm. And it leaves the reader poised at the edge of the tragedy, every reasonable word having been spoken, waiting only for the youth's refusal — recorded in the next verse — to turn Abner's reluctant warning into a spear-thrust he did all he could to avoid.
23:
Asahel's refusal meets its end, and the witnesses read the verse with a shared sense of pity and of hard justice. John Gill, the reformed voice, states the young man's resolve: "he refused to turn aside," being "determined on making him his captive if possible." The fatal obstinacy the commentators have traced since v.19 now runs its course, and Abner, "with the hinder end of the spear," smites him so that "the spear came out behind him, and he fell down there, and died in the same place."

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch describe the blow with care: only "when he still refused to depart in spite of this warning" did Abner wound him "in the abdomen with the hinder part, i.e., the lower end of the spear," which "appears to have been pointed, that it might" be driven into the ground when at rest. Matthew Henry, at v.18, had already drawn the meaning the whole tradition presses. Asahel "thought that Abner spoke so courteously because he feared him," and so ran on; but Abner "gave him his death's wound with a back stroke," a "pass which Asahel was not acquainted with, nor had learned to stand upon his guard against." Henry's twofold lesson governs the reading: "how death often comes upon us by ways that we least suspect" — "who would fear the hand of a flying enemy or the butt-end of a spear?" — and "how we are often betrayed by the accomplishments we are proud of," for Asahel's swiftness "forwarded his fate, and with it he ran upon his death, instead of running from it."

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley echoes the very words: "So Asahel's swiftness, which he presumed on so much, only forwarded his fate! With it he ran upon his death, instead of running from it." The witnesses do not divide; they read Asahel's fall as a warning written in blood against presuming upon a natural gift. Henry also marks the effect of the death upon the field — that "all that came to the place stood still," the pursuit checked by the sight of the fallen champion — even as Joab and Abishai, "instead of being disheartened, were exasperated by it," and pressed on the harder. Thus the single stroke that ends Asahel both halts the chase for a moment and kindles the vengeance that will pursue Abner to his own grave, so that the death of one swift young man becomes the seed of a longer, bitterer reckoning between the houses of Joab and Abner.
24:
The pursuit resumes with fresh fury, and the witnesses read the verse as the moment Asahel's death turns the chase into a vendetta. John Gill, the reformed voice, corrects the sense of the opening word: "or rather but Joab, and Abishai, pursued after Abner." Where the rest "stood still" at the sight of their fallen comrade, these two brothers, "filled with indignation and resentment, pursued after Abner, to be avenged on him." The chase is now personal, driven by blood-loyalty rather than the fortunes of the war.

The grammatical-historical commentators fix the geography and the failing light. Keil and Delitzsch record that "Joab and Abishai pursued Abner till the sun set, and until they had arrived at the hill Ammah, in front of Giah, on the way to the desert of Gibeon," confessing that "nothing further is known of the places mentioned here." Charles Ellicott agrees that "no identification of either Ammah or Giah has yet been made," but reasons that since it lay "by the way of the wilderness of Gibeon," the site "was not far from that town, and hence that the pursuit was not long." The setting sun, on their reading, is not idle scenery; it marks the natural limit of the day's bloodshed and prepares the parley the next verses record, for the coming night "would oblige them to retire," as Henry observes at v.18.

The witnesses do not divide over this verse; they agree that it carries the reader from the general rout into the narrowing personal chase that Asahel's fall has kindled. What began as an aggression by Abner and a defensive response by David's men has become, through the death of one youth, a pursuit charged with the fiercest of motives. Yet the very details the commentators cannot fully place — the unknown hill, the unnamed Giah, the wilderness road — lend the scene its air of a chase pressed to the edge of exhaustion and daylight, the brothers driving Abner across broken country until the sun fails them. And it is precisely this limit, the gathering dark and the mounting cost, that will give Abner his opening to call for a halt. The verse thus poises the story between vengeance and restraint: Joab and Abishai burn to avenge their brother, but the day is nearly spent, and the Benjamites are even now rallying to Abner on a hilltop, ready to turn and make a desperate stand.
25:
Abner rallies the remnant of his beaten force and begs for a halt, and the witnesses read the verse as a study in how defeat changes a man's tune. John Gill, the reformed commentator, pictures the Benjamites gathering "after Abner" — either those "dispersed" in the rout now regrouped, or fresh men come "out of the several cities" — until they "became one troop, and stood on the top of an hill," a strong position from which to "beat back their pursuers." Charles Ellicott, the grammatical-historical voice, adds that while "the rest of Abner's force appears to have been hopelessly scattered," he "succeeded in rallying the Benjamites, his own and Saul's kinsmen, in a strong position."

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry gives the verse its moral edge, and it is one of the chapter's sharpest ironies. Abner, "being conquered, meanly begs for a cessation of arms." The change of note is the whole point: "He that was most forward to fight was the first that had enough of it. He that made a jest of bloodshed — 'Let the young men arise and play before us,' v.14 — is now shocked at it, when he finds himself on the losing side." In the morning it was but playing with the sword; now his cry is, "Shall the sword devour for ever?" Henry draws two lessons the tradition repeats: "how easy it is for men to use reason when it makes for them, who would not use it if it made against them" — had Abner been the conqueror, "we should not have had him complaining of the voraciousness of the sword" — and "how the issue of things alters men's minds," so that "the same thing which looked pleasant in the morning at night looked dismal."

Abner's plea itself, which the next verse will quote in full, appeals to kinship and to consequences: they were "brethren, who ought not thus to bite and devour one another," and civil war "will be bitterness in the latter end," a phrase Henry links to Israel's grief after the war with Benjamin (Judges 21:2). The witnesses do not divide; they read Abner's sudden eloquence as the self-serving prudence of a man now on the losing side, and they turn it to a general warning. "Those that are forward to enter into contention," Henry writes with Solomon, "will perhaps repent it before they have done with it, and therefore had better leave it off before it be meddled with" — for it is true of every sin "that it will be bitterness in the latter end." The maker of the quarrel is the first to sue for peace.
26:
Abner's cry from the hilltop — "Shall the sword devour for ever?" — is read by the witnesses both for its plea and for the motives that may lie behind it. John Gill, the reformed voice, notes the practical circumstance that made the appeal possible: "having now a troop of men with him, he could stop with the greater safety," and, being "on an hill, and perhaps Joab on one opposite to him, could call to him, so as to be heard." The parley is spoken across a valley between two heights.

The substance of Abner's words, Keil and Delitzsch explain, is a warning against a fight to the death: "Shall the sword then devour for ever? dost thou not know that bitterness arises at last?" Thus Abner "warns Joab of the consequences of a desperate struggle, and calls upon him to put an end to all further bloodshed by suspending the pursuit." The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley draws out the appeal to kinship: the coming "bitterness" means "it will produce dreadful effects," and the combatants are "brethren, by nation and religion, whom therefore they should not pursue with so fierce a rage, as if they were pursuing the Philistines." The argument is that Israelite should not devour Israelite as though he were a heathen foe.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott probes what may lie beneath the plea. "It may be," he suggests, "that Abner was already considering the expediency of transferring his allegiance to the house of David, or, at least, had had enough experience of Ish-bosheth to see that it would be impossible to unite the tribes under his sway." At the very least, Ellicott adds, "his sense of the disastrous effects of civil war was doubtless quickened by his own defeat and present danger" — a candid acknowledgment that the appeal comes conveniently from the losing side.

The witnesses do not sharply divide, but they hold two readings in tension: Abner's words are true, and they are also self-interested. The truth of them is plain — civil war among brethren is a devouring evil that ends in bitterness, and the point had already been pressed by Henry at v.25. Yet the timing is telling, for the man who "made a jest of bloodshed" in the morning discovers its horror only when the sword turns against him. Ellicott's suggestion that Abner may already be weighing a change of side casts the plea forward to the intrigues of the next chapter, where Abner will indeed seek terms with David. For now the verse leaves Joab to answer, and the reader to judge whether the appeal is the fruit of conscience, of calculation, or of both at once.
27:
Joab answers Abner's plea with an oath, and the witnesses divide gently over exactly what he means by it. John Gill, the reformed voice, simply marks the solemnity: "As God liveth" was "the form of an oath, swearing by the living God." Upon the meaning of what follows, however, the commentators offer two readings.

The first, favoured by the wesleyan-arminian voices, takes Joab to be fixing the blame for the whole battle upon Abner. Adam Clarke paraphrases: "If Abner had not provoked the battle" — the challenge of v.14 — "Joab would not have attacked the Israelites that day, as his orders were probably to act on the defensive; therefore the blame fell upon Israel." John Wesley presses the same sense: "Unless thou hadst made the motion that they should fight, it was thou, not I, that gave the first occasion of this fight. Abner was the sole cause of this war; otherwise all things had been ended by an amicable agreement, which might have been made that very morning, if he had so pleased." On this reading Joab throws the guilt of the bloodshed squarely back on the man who now sues for peace. Keil and Delitzsch agree, glossing Joab's oath as the claim that "if thou hadst not spoken, i.e., challenged to single combat, the people would have gone away in the morning, every one from his brother — there would have been no such fratricidal conflict at all," so that "Joab threw all the blame of the fight upon Abner."

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott records both this and a second possibility. Joab's reply, he says, admits "either of two interpretations": either Joab "seeks to throw the whole blame of the conflict upon Abner," or — "as the phrase is more generally and more probably understood" — Joab means "that he had intended to keep up the pursuit only until the following morning, but as Abner already sued for mercy, he was content, and would stop now." The one reading makes the verse an accusation; the other makes it a concession.

The witnesses do not quarrel bitterly; the difference is one of nuance in a single Hebrew oath. Yet both readings agree on the outcome and on the moral. Whether Joab is chiefly reproaching Abner or chiefly granting his request, he lays the origin of the day's slaughter at Abner's door — the man "forward to make mischief," in Henry's phrase at v.25, being "commonly the first to complain of it." And whichever sense is taken, Joab consents to halt, so that the trumpet of the next verse ends the pursuit and lets Abner's broken force withdraw.
28:
Joab sounds the retreat, and the witnesses read the verse as the generous close of a bloody day — mercy shown by the victor at his master's mind. John Gill, the reformed commentator, notes that Joab "blew a trumpet, or caused one to be blown, as a signal of a retreat," and "all the people stood still, and pursued after Israel no more." Matthew Henry, at v.25, had already drawn the character of the act: though "a conqueror," Joab "generously grants" the cessation, "knowing very well his master's mind and how averse he was to the shedding of blood." He might have "pushed on his victory, and made a full end of Abner's forces," but "like one that pitied the mistake of his adversaries, he very honourably, by sound of trumpet, put a stop to the pursuit," so that "it is good husbandry to be sparing of blood."

The grammatical-historical commentators trace Abner's night retreat and mark the campaign's limit. Keil and Delitzsch follow him "through the Arabah, i.e., the valley of the Jordan, marching the whole night," then across the river "through the whole of Bithron back to Mahanaim." Bithron, they argue, "is not the name of a city, but of a district," east of the Jordan, "only mentioned here"; the word means "a cutting," and they resist identifying it with Bethhoron. Charles Ellicott glosses the closing words "neither fought they any more" with a needed qualification: it means only "in this present campaign," for 2 Samuel 3:1 records that "there was a long war between the house of Saul and the house of David." The truce, that is, ends a battle, not the war.

The witnesses do not divide; they read the trumpet-blast as a deliberate restraint that reflects David's own policy. Joab, obedient to a king "who sought the welfare of all Israel and therefore not the hurt of any," spares the beaten remnant and lets Abner make an orderly withdrawal. The mercy is real, and the commentators praise it; yet Ellicott's caveat keeps the reader from mistaking it for peace. The sword is sheathed for a night, and the two armies march wearily back the way they came — Abner over Jordan to Mahanaim, Joab toward Hebron — but the deeper quarrel between the two houses is only suspended. The chapter's closing verses will tally the dead and bury Asahel, and the "long war" Ellicott foresees will grind on until the house of David, as the whole book moves to show, at last prevails.
29:
Abner's weary night-march homeward closes the day's fighting, and the witnesses read the verse for both its geography and its ominous place in the larger story. John Gill, the reformed voice, gives the reason for the haste: Abner "marched with his men all night, lest Joab should return, and pursue him, and take vengeance on him for the death of his brother." Even under truce, the shadow of Asahel's blood drives the retreat, for Abner has cause to fear the avenger.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott traces the route with care. "The wilderness of Gibeon lay to the east of the town, and Abner's flight had thus carried him towards the Jordan"; he now "passed up the valley of the Jordan" — the Arabah — "and, crossing at a ford, went through all Bithron to Mahanaim." Like Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott takes Bithron for "the name of a district on the east of the Jordan," acknowledging it "is not further known." The march thus returns Abner to the very city from which he had set out at v.12, his aggression against Judah ending where it began, but with his army bloodied and his cause weakened.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke lifts the verse to its historical significance. Abner and his men "came to Mahanaim, so they returned to the place whence they set out"; but Clarke sees in the day just past a fateful beginning: "This was the commencement of the civil wars between Israel and Judah, and properly the commencement of the division of the two kingdoms, through which both nations were deluged with blood." The skirmish at Gibeon, that is, is no isolated clash but the opening of a long and bitter rift.

The witnesses do not divide; they agree in reading the retreat as both an ending and a portent. The immediate fighting is over, and Abner slips back across Jordan under cover of night, hurrying to outrun a possible pursuit. Yet the commentators will not let the reader rest in the quiet of the march. Clarke's grave observation frames the whole scene: what looks like a single defeated general going home is in truth the first stroke of a division that will "deluge both nations with blood." The night road to Mahanaim leads back not to peace but to the "long war" of 2 Samuel 3:1, and the reader is left to feel, beneath the exhausted tramp of Abner's column, the weight of the strife that David's patience must yet endure before the throne is one.
30:
The battle over, the historian counts the cost, and the witnesses attend to the tally and to the disparity it reveals. John Gill, the reformed commentator, notes the motive in Joab's turning back — it was "in his commission from David to shed as little blood as he could" — so that even the muster of the dead is coloured by David's restraint. When Joab "had gathered all the people together, there lacked of David's servants nineteen men and Asahel."

The grammatical-historical commentators dwell on why so few of David's men fell against so many of Israel's. Keil and Delitzsch mark "this striking disproportion" — nineteen and Asahel against three hundred and sixty — and account for it by the quality of the two armies: "in Joab's army there were none but brave and well-tried men, who had gathered round David a long time before, whereas in Abner's army there were only the remnants of the Israelites who had been beaten upon Gilboa," men "still further weakened and depressed" by their struggle to recover land held by the Philistines. Charles Ellicott offers the same explanation and adds a second cause drawn from the tactics of ancient war. He notes first that Joab "cannot be supposed to have returned that day farther than to Gibeon, since it was already sunset before the pursuit ended," and there "mustered his forces, and counted and buried the slain." The great disparity, he judges, "is to be accounted for partly by the advantage given by bow and spear, the chief weapons of ancient warfare, to the pursuer over the pursued, and partly by the fact that Joab's men had been long trained under David in hardship and deeds of valour, while Abner's men were the remnants of Saul's defeated army." Ellicott also raises, but leaves open, a small question of reckoning: "it is uncertain whether these numbers include the twelve champion combatants on each side."

The witnesses do not divide; they read the lopsided count as a quiet vindication of David's cause. The single name lifted out of the nineteen — "and Asahel" — is set apart because, as Henry observed at v.25, he "was worth more than all"; the historian will not let the swift young warrior be lost in a bare total. Yet the numbers as a whole tell their own tale: the tried men of David, few of them fallen, prevail over the depleted host of a rival king. The commentators find in this no mere accident of arms but a token of the providence that has run through the chapter — the house that God chose losing least, and the house that man set up losing most, as the long contest tilts, verse by verse and battle by battle, toward the throne David was promised.
31:
The historian records the number of Israel's dead, and the witnesses read it as the measure of David's victory and the mark of a war grimly begun. John Gill, the reformed voice, states the plain sense: "of those of the tribe of Benjamin that joined him, and of those that he brought with him from Mahanaim," so many were slain that "three hundred and threescore men died" — the toll evident, Gill notes, "by their bodies lying on the field of battle." The disproportion with David's nineteen, already weighed at v.30, is here made stark.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch carry the verse straight on into its sequel, joining the count of the fallen to the burial that follows: "the servants of David had smitten of Benjamin, and of Abner's men, so that three hundred and threescore men died. And they took up Asahel, and buried him in the sepulchre of his father, which was in Bethlehem. And Joab and his men went all night, and they came to Hebron at break of day." The number, in their reading, is not left to stand alone but is folded into the weary homeward march of David's men, who bear their one honoured corpse to its grave even as they leave the enemy's dead upon the field.

The witnesses do not divide over this verse; the figure speaks for itself, and they let it. Yet in setting the three hundred and sixty of Israel against the nineteen of Judah, the commentators find the same lesson the chapter has been teaching from the first: that the tried and valiant men who had gathered to David long before now prevail decisively over the depleted remnant of Saul's beaten army. The count is a token of where the favour of God rests. At the same time, the sheer size of Israel's loss underscores the tragedy Abner himself lamented on the hilltop — that the sword had "devoured" among brethren, and that this was, as Clarke observed at v.29, "the commencement of the civil wars between Israel and Judah." The bare number, then, carries a double weight: it vindicates the house of David, and it mourns the blood of Israelites shed by Israelites, three hundred and sixty men fallen in a quarrel that Abner made and that David's patience must yet outlast before the two houses are one.
32:
The chapter closes with the burial of Asahel and the weary homeward march, and the witnesses read the ending both for its historical detail and for the meditation on death it invites. John Gill, the reformed commentator, follows Josephus in taking it that Joab and Abishai, "his brethren, after they had buried the dead in the field of battle, took up" Asahel from the place where he fell, to carry him to a more honoured grave.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott fills in the geography and the timing. The bodies of the ordinary soldiers "were probably buried on the spot, but on account of Asahel's position and near relationship to David, his body was carried to Bethlehem, for burial in the sepulchre of his father." From this Ellicott infers a small biographical fact — "that Zeruiah's husband, of whom there is no other mention, was also of Bethlehem" — and he reconstructs the march: the burial "must have taken place on the next day," and, with "the previous march of ten miles, would have filled up that day," so that "it was twenty-four hours after the close of the battle before they were ready to start from Bethlehem." The final leg to Hebron, "about thirteen miles," was made by night, "chosen for the march to avoid the heat." Keil and Delitzsch render the closing clause exactly: "it got light to them at Hebron," so that "they reached Hebron itself at daybreak."

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, whose comment on this verse stands in his block at v.25, lifts the burial into its lasting lesson. The common dead were buried "in the field of battle, but Asahel was carried to Bethlehem, and buried in the sepulchre of his father." From this distinction between the graves of some and of others Henry draws his consolation: "Thus are distinctions made between the dust of some and that of others; but in the resurrection no other difference will be made but that between godly and ungodly, which will remain for ever." The honours we pay the dead, that is, are real but temporary; the one division that abides is moral, not social.

The witnesses do not divide; they close the chapter on a note at once sober and hopeful. Asahel, the swift young hero undone by his own courage, is carried home to lie with his fathers, while the nameless slain are left where they fell — and the commentators, refusing to end merely in the dust of battle, point past the sepulchre to the resurrection, where the only reckoning that finally matters will be made. So the day of Gibeon ends: the war unfinished, the dead buried, and David's men trudging through the dark to reach Hebron at the break of day.
